data.title: Earnings Flash (JBI) Janus International Group Reports Q1 Adjusted EPS $0.13
Norges Bank in No Hurry to Cut Interest RatesMarket Talk
data.description: Norges Bank is in no hurry to cut interest rates due to the combination of high inflation and continued moderate economic growth, Sydbank senior economist Kim Blindbaek writes. However, uncertainty is high. If Trump suddenly escalates the trade conflict, a rate cut could be brought forward, Sydbank says. Tariff threats will probably send the global economy into a lower gear. At the same time, goods that were previously intended for the U.S. market may now head to Europe, Sydbank says. This could push down the price of goods in Europe and suggests that inflation will be lower in European countries. "We estimate that Norges Bank will cut interest rates for the first time at the September interest rate meeting and once more this year." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1056 GMT - Norges Bank is in no hurry to cut interest rates due to the combination of high inflation and continued moderate economic growth, Sydbank senior economist Kim Blindbaek writes. However, uncertainty is high. If Trump suddenly escalates the trade conflict, a rate cut could be brought forward, Sydbank says. Tariff threats will probably send the global economy into a lower gear. At the same time, goods that were previously intended for the U.S. market may now head to Europe, Sydbank says. This could push down the price of goods in Europe and suggests that inflation will be lower in European countries. "We estimate that Norges Bank will cut interest rates for the first time at the September interest rate meeting and once more this year." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1054 GMT - The Riksbank has suggested the possibility of a slight easing of monetary policy going forward, but Nordea expects the central bank to stay on hold, Nordea chief analyst Torbjorn Isaksson writes. The decision to keep rates steady earlier Thursday was widely expected and policymakers also signaled an unchanged policy rate ahead of the next monetary policy meeting in June, Nordea says. The Riksbank sees downside risks to inflation and growth, and emphasized its readiness to ease monetary policy if necessary. However, based on Nordea's inflation forecasts and its view that the Swedish economy is relatively resilient, it expects the Riksbank will keep the policy rate unchanged at 2.25% further out. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1037 GMT - The 10-year maturity segment of the U.S. Treasury curve could outperform other tenors given their relative underperformance so far this year, HSBC rates strategists say in a note. The 10-year tenor, despite a 27 basis point decline in yields year to date, has relatively lagged the shift lower at both the two- and five-year maturities so far this year, they say. "This, in our view, presents scope for outperformance of Treasurys further out the curve as the easing cycle plays out and lingering risk premium from the early April volatility dissipates but it may take time to materialize," they say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1030 GMT - HSBC maintains a neutral duration conviction on U.S. Treasurys, waiting for some of the policy uncertainty to subside, while seeking better value elsewhere, its strategists say in a note. "Given the prevailing policy uncertainties and mixed economic data that has done little to challenge the Federal Reserve's 'wait and see' narrative, better near-term value may be found in other developed market bonds," they say. Still, HSBC continues to expect meaningfully lower Treasury yields by year-end. The strategists maintain their end-2025 forecast of 3.50% for the 10-year Treasury yield. The 10-year Treasury yield last trades at 4.308%, up around 3 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1029 GMT - The Bank of England is expected to cut interest rates by 25 basis points during the policy rate decision due at 1102 GMT and is likely to keep options open for further cuts in 2025, London Capital Group's Matt Basi says in a note. Increased uncertainty around geopolitics and macroeconomic data are likely to raise the possibility of further BOE interest-rate cuts in the coming months, Basi says. Markets price in a total of 95 basis points of BOE interest rate cuts by the end of 2025, LSEG data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - Norges Bank is keeping its cards close to its chest, repeating that the policy rate will likely be cut sometime this year, Handelsbanken's Marius Gonsholt Hov writes. The message was as expected, in addition to the decision to keep the main policy rate unchanged at 4.50%. The market sees a roughly 50/50 chance of a rate cut in June, but Handelsbanken believes that would be too early. Unemployment remains low, price pressures remain strong, and the krone is significantly weaker than Norges Bank estimates. "Unless we see signs that unemployment is rising more quickly than anticipated and/or that inflation falls more rapidly, we maintain our view that Norges Bank will hold the rate steady until autumn," he says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0936 GMT - The rise in German industrial production, together with the strong increase in orders, indicates the country's manufacturing sector is gradually picking up, Commerzbank's Ralph Solveen says in a note. Industrial output climbed 3% on month in March, after orders increased 3.6%. Production in energy-intensive sectors has stabilized, and the auto industry has made up for the slump in production at the end of 2024, he says. While the data exaggerate the underlying trend in production--as production was brought forward due to impending U.S. tariffs--the trend should be upward in the coming months. However, a strong upturn is unlikely due to U.S. tariff policies and "the numerous structural problems facing the German economy," he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0935 GMT - The cost of insuring U.K. government debt against default using credit default swaps decline to their lowest since before U.S. President Trump announced widespread trade tariffs in early April. The moves comes amid reports that a trade deal between the U.S. and the U.K. could be announced later Thursday after President Trump posted on social media about an imminent trade agreement. It also comes ahead of a Bank of England interest rate decision at 1102 GMT, where a 25 basis-point rate cut is expected. The U.K. 5-year credit default swap falls 1 basis point to 20bps, a 5-week low, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0928 GMT - Bank Negara Malaysia is expected to keep its policy rate steady at 3.00% through 2025, but there is a risk of a 25bps cut in 2H if GDP growth dips below 4%, RHB economists Chin Yee Sian and Wong Xian Yong say in a note. They think the policy decision will depend on growth momentum, inflation and global interest rate trends. They see inflation remaining manageable, with headline CPI potentially at 2.2%, supported by easing global commodity prices and subdued domestic demand pressures. While domestic demand should remain resilient, external risks from trade tensions and tariffs may cloud the outlook, they add. RHB cuts Malaysia's 2025 growth forecast to 4.5% from the previously expected 5.0%, with risks skewed to the downside if trade tensions escalate. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0927 GMT - If the Federal Reserve were to cut interest rates prematurely, the 10-year Treasury yield could move higher, says Brandywine Global's Bill Zox in a note. Typically yields fall when interest rates are cut, but an early rate cut would be counterproductive, the portfolio manager says. Such a move could raise concerns that the Fed is losing control of inflation. It could also spark worries that the Fed is yielding to political pressure after President Trump recently criticized the Fed for not cutting rates. The real action to watch is between the Trump administration and the financial markets, Zox says. The 10-year Tresaury yield trades at 4.308%, up 3 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0917 GMT - Sweden's Riksbank indicated that a rate cut is likely this year given the weakness of the economy, and Capital Economics thinks it will probably cut the policy rate in August by 25 basis points to 2.0%. The policy statement noted the Swedish economy has been a bit weaker than expected and the outlook has deteriorated, prompting policymakers to indicate they might ease monetary policy slightly, analyst Adrian Prettejohn writes. Market consensus is for a further 50 basis points of cuts this year, with the next move in August. "We do not think that the Riksbank will cut that far as there are signs that some of the surge in core inflation this year is due to underlying inflationary pressures, rather than just one-off effects." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0915 GMT - Bank Negara Malaysia appears to have shifted to a more dovish tone, suggesting room for easing, says HSBC Asean economist Yun Liu in a note. "As a central bank that is cautious about changing its qualitative language, BNM has made multiple adjustments to its growth stance, flagging mounting headwinds," she notes. While inflation concerns have eased, the central bank now seems more focused on the downside risks to growth, she adds. HSBC expects a 25bp rate cut in July, while noting the outlook remains fluid and dependent on incoming data to gauge the impact of U.S. tariffs on the Malaysian economy. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Flutter Entertainment Launches of Third Tranche of Shr Buyback Program
BCE Cancels Discounted Treasury Issuance for Dividend Reinvestment Plan
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

BCE said it will stop issuing common shares from the treasury at a discounted rate as part of its shareholder dividend reinvestment and stock purchase plan.

The Canadian telecom giant said that instead of its discounted DRP program, which offered a 2% discount to the average market price, shares will instead be purchased by BCE's agent, TSX Trust Company, on the secondary market with cash provided by BCE.

The modifications will take effect alongside the dividend payment on July 15.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: US bulls say economy will boom no matter what
data.description:
Key points:
  • Stoxx 600 up 0.7%, S&P futures up 1%
  • Siemens Energy at new record high after results
  • Bank of England rate decision due later
  • UK-US trade deal announcement also expected

US BULLS SAY ECONOMY WILL BOOM NO MATTER WHAT

Trump's tariff announcement last month caused widespread recession alarm, with hedge funds rushing out of the market as indices sold off and volatility gauges echoed moves last seen at the height of the COVID shock in 2020.

Despite the prevailing doom-and-gloom narrative accompanying these wild swings - which also saw Wall Street banks scrambling to cut their growth forecasts to bake in higher tariffs - others were keeping cool about the prospects for the U.S. economy.

Back during the early April selloff, Nouriel Roubini, CEO of Roubini Macro Associates and professor emeritus at New York University, outlined a bullish outlook for the U.S., saying technological advancements would offset new trade barriers.

"With the momentum that innovation brings, it won't even matter if Mickey Mouse is president - the economy will keep growing," Roubini was quoted as saying. "By the end of this decade, U.S. growth could hit 4%."

Siding with Roubini's "Mickey Mouse argument", Pareto Asset Management Chief Economist and Strategist Finn Øystein Bergh also believes there is no reason to lose sleep over trade war concerns, pointing to the underlying strength of the private sector.

"There is of course a real chance of both recession and stagflation... Given the strength of the business sector, however, and perhaps a modicum of common sense in the Trump administration, I believe it is not likely to be severe".

He says the big unresolved issue in the U.S. economy is the government deficit, which has grown from 65% of GDP in 2007 to over 120% today, while the private sector is in much better shape, having deleveraged heavily since the financial crisis.

"I put my trust in firms with sound balance sheets, capable leadership, and resilient business models," writes Bergh in his latest monthly note.

(Danilo Masoni)

*****

data.title: Prestige Consumer Healthcare Fiscal Q4 Adjusted Net Income, Revenue Increase; Fiscal 2026 Outlook Issued
data.description:
Prestige Consumer Healthcare reported fiscal Q4 adjusted net income Thursday of $1.32 per diluted share, up from $1.02 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $1.30.

Revenue for the quarter ended March 31 was $296.5 million, up from $277 million a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $291.1 million.

The consumer drug provider said it expects fiscal 2026 diluted EPS of $4.70 to $4.82 on revenue of $1.14 billion to $1.16 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet expect EPS of $4.79 on revenue of $1.16 billion.

Shares of the company were down more than 2.5% in recent premarket activity.

data.title: REG - RWS Holdings PLC - Director/PDMR Shareholding
data.description:
RNS Number : 9137H RWS Holdings PLC 08 May 2025  

8 May 2025

 RWS Holdings plc

Notification and public disclosure of transactions by PDMRs

RWS Holdings plc ("RWS" or "the Company"), a unique world-leading provider of technology-enabled language, content and intellectual property services, announces that Vasagi Kothandapani, President, Enterprise Services, has acquired 96,266 ordinary shares of 1 pence each in the Company ("Shares"), as detailed below.

This announcement is made in accordance with Article 19(3) of the EU Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014 (as incorporated into UK domestic law by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018).

 For further information, please contact:

RWS Holdings plc

Julie Southern, Chairman

Benjamin Faes, Chief Executive Officer

01628 410100

MHP (Financial PR advisor)

Katie Hunt / Eleni Menikou

rws@mhpgroup.com

020 3128 8100

07884 494112

Deutsche Numis (Nomad & Joint Broker)

Stuart Skinner / William Wickham

020 7260 1000

Berenberg (Joint Broker)

Ben Wright / Toby Flaux / Mike Burke / Milo Bonser

020 3207 7800

About RWS:

RWS Holdings plc is a unique, world-leading provider of technology-enabled language, content and intellectual property services. Through content transformation and multilingual data analysis, our combination of AI-enabled technology and human expertise helps our clients to grow by ensuring they are understood anywhere, in any language.

Our purpose is unlocking global understanding. By combining cultural understanding, client understanding and technical understanding, our services and technology assist our clients to acquire and retain customers, deliver engaging user experiences, maintain compliance and gain actionable insights into their data and content.

Over the past 20 years we've been evolving our own AI solutions as well as helping clients to explore, build and use multilingual AI applications. With 45+ AI-related patents and more than 100 peer-reviewed papers, we have the experience and expertise to support clients on their AI journey.

We work with over 80% of the world's top 100 brands, more than three-quarters of Fortune's 20 'Most Admired Companies' and almost all of the top pharmaceutical companies, investment banks, law firms and patent filers. Our client base spans Europe, Asia Pacific, Africa and North and South America. Our 60+ global locations across five continents service clients in the automotive, chemical, financial, legal, medical, pharmaceutical, technology and telecommunications sectors.

Founded in 1958, RWS is headquartered in the UK and publicly listed on AIM, the London Stock Exchange regulated market (RWS.L). 

For further information, please visit www.rws.com

data.title: Earnings Flash (USFD) US Foods Holding Posts Q1 Revenue $9.35B, vs. FactSet Est of $9.38B
U.S. Raven, Serbia's PR-DC to jointly make IKA-Bomber drones
data.description:
BELGRADE (Serbia), May 8 (SeeNews) - U.S. firm Raven Autonomous Technologies said it has established a joint venture with Serbian aerospace company PR-DC for the production of the IKA-Bomber rotary wing drone and its delivery to global markets.

The partnership will combine Raven’s leading-edge autonomous technologies with PR-DC’s robust military-grade designs to manufacture the Serbia-made aerial platform, with plans for U.S.-based production as well, Raven said in a statement last week.

"The IKA-Bomber is a durable and powerful rotary wing drone built in a hexacopter configuration. Constructed from advanced composite materials including carbon fiber and epoxy resin, the drone offers unmatched strength and lightweight performance. Capable of 40+ minute flights while carrying 20 kg, the IKA-Bomber sets a new standard in aerial logistics and military operations," as per the statement.

The drone can autonomously deploy 12 60mm NATO mortar rounds, which can be released one-by-one or simultaneously to neutralise multiple targets effectively and efficiently.

Raven Autonomous Technologies says on its website it provides training and operational integration for unmanned systems/countermeasures.

PR-DC provides full in-house research and development in the aerospace sector - from design and verification to rolling out the final product, the company says on its website. In 2024, it opened the first Serbian drone factory, marking the beginning of serial drone manufacturing in the Balkan country.
